author: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy
book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: I will briefly set forth the manner of life according to these virtues, one by one, after I have brought forward, as I promised, passages from the Old Testament parallel to those I have been quoting from the New Testament.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: For is Paul alone in saying that we should be joined to God so that there should be nothing between to separate us?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Does not the prophet say the same most aptly and concisely in the words, "It is good for me to cleave to God?"

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Does not this one word cleave express all that the apostle says at length about love?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: And do not the words, It is good, point to the apostle’s statement, "All things issue in good to them that love God?"

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Thus in one clause and in two words the prophet sets forth the power and the fruit of love.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: And as the apostle says that the Son of God is the virtue of God and the wisdom of God,—virtue being understood to refer to action, and wisdom to teaching (as in the gospel these two things are expressed in the words, "All things were made by Him," which belongs to action and virtue; and then, referring to teaching and the knowledge of the truth, he says, "The life was the light of men"),—could anything agree better with these passages than what is said in the Old Testament of wisdom, "She reaches from end to end in strength, and orders all things sweetly?"

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: For reaching in strength expresses virtue, while ordering sweetly expresses skill and method.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: But if this seems obscure, see what follows: "And of all," he says, "God loved her; for she teaches the knowledge of God, and chooses His works."

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Nothing more is found here about action; for choosing works is not the same as working, so this refers to teaching.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: There remains action to correspond with the virtue, to complete the truth we wish to prove.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Read then what comes next: "But if," he says, "the possession which is desired in life is honorable, what is more honorable than wisdom, which works all things?"

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Could anything be brought forward more striking or more distinct than this, or even more fully expressed?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Or, if you wish more, hear another passage of the same meaning.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: "Wisdom," he says, "teaches sobriety, and justice, and virtue."

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Sobriety refers, I think, to the knowledge of the truth, or to teaching; justice and virtue to work and action.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: And I know nothing comparable to these two things, that is, to efficiency in action and sobriety in contemplation, which the virtue of God and the wisdom of God, that is, the Son of God, gives to them that love Him, when the same prophet goes on to show their value; for it is thus stated: "Wisdom teaches sobriety, and justice, and virtue, than which nothing is more useful in life to man."

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Perhaps some may think that those passages do not refer to the Son of God.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: What, then, is taught in the following words: "She displays the nobility of her birth, having her dwelling with God?"

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: To what does birth refer but to parentage?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: And does not dwelling with the Father claim and assert equality?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Again, as Paul says that the Son of God is the wisdom of God, and as the Lord Himself says, "No man knoweth the Father save the only-begotten Son," what could be more concordant than those words of the prophet: "With Thee is wisdom which knows Thy works, which was present at the time of Thy making the world, and knew what would be pleasing in Thine eyes?"

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: And as Christ is called the truth, which is also taught by His being called the brightness of the Father (for there is nothing round about the sun but its brightness which is produced from it), what is there in the Old Testament more plainly and obviously in accordance with this than the words, "Thy truth is round about Thee?"

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Once more, Wisdom herself says in the gospel, "No man cometh unto the Father but by me;" and the prophet says, "Who knoweth Thy mind, unless Thou givest wisdom?" and a little after, "The things pleasing to Thee men have learned, and have been healed by wisdom."

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Paul says, "The love of God is shed abroad in our hearts by the Holy Spirit which is given unto us;" and the prophet says, "The Holy Spirit of knowledge will shun guile."

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: For where there is guile there is no love.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Paul says that we are "conformed to the image of the Son of God;" and the prophet says, "The light of Thy countenance is stamped upon us."

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Paul teaches that the Holy Spirit is God, and therefore is no creature; and the prophet says, "Thou sendest Thy Spirit from the higher."

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: For God alone is the highest, than whom nothing is higher.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Paul shows that the Trinity is one God, when he says, "To Him be glory;" and in the Old Testament it is said, "Hear, O Israel, the Lord thy God is one God."
